coredata.dc:title: Introduction of porous structure via facile carbon-dot modulation: A feasible and promising approach for improving the photocatalytic capability of sulfur doped g-C3N4
coredata.prism:issueName: Advances in photo(electro)catalysis for environmental applications and chemical synthesis
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shiping
Zhao, Yu
Sun, Xiang
Zheng, Xuyang
Ni, Shouqing
Yue, Qinyan
Gao, Baoyu
coredata.dc:description: g-C3N4 with uniformly porous structure is very attractive owing to its special surface and interface properties in photocatalytic field. Herein, uniformly porous sulfur doped C3N4/carbon dot (S-C3N4/C-dot) composite with narrow pore size distribution was successfully synthesized via a facile one-step calcination of S-C3N4 precursor with the prefabricated C-dot, rather than employing any other template materials. Experiments found, it was the C-dot that served as the pore-forming agent via its own oxidation into gaseous products, and ratio of C-dot in the raw material played a key role in controlling the crystal structure, morphology and photocatalytic property of the final products. With the optimum ratio, the obtained S-C3N4/C-dot composite exhibited a superior activity in antibiotics degradation compared with pure S-C3N4, which was attributed to the unique pore structure, large specific surface area, strong visible light absorption and less recombination of electron-hole pairs. In addition, trapping experiments and intermediate product analyses were also carried out to understand the antibiotics removal mechanism and pathways. This study is very helpful to deeply understand the function of C-dot decoration, and declare the great potential of g-C3N4 in practical water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uniformly porous g-C3N4
Carbon dot
Template-Free synthesis
optimum ratio

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of S-C3N4/C-dot composites synthesis.
XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of S-5 sample, and high-resolution spectrum of (b) C 1 s in S-0 and S-5, (c) S 2p in S-5, and (d) N 1 s in S-5 sample.
TEM images of (a) C-dot, (b) S-0, (c) S-1, (d) S-3, (e) S-5 and (f) S-8.
(a)Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and (b)the corresponding pore size distribution curves of all samples.
(a) UV-vis absorption spectra, and (b) Kubelka–Munk transformed spectra of all samples, and (c) Mott-Schottky plots of S-0 and S-5 samples.
Transient photocurrent response of all samples (inset: the average current density of all the samples in the first cycle).
Photocatalytic degradation of SMX with different photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (inset: SMX removal efficiency of all samples within 180 min).
Reusability performance of S-5 towards photocatalytic SMX degradation.
Photodegradation of SMX in active species trapping experiment with S-5 as photocatalyst.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism over S-5 sample under visible light irradiation.
The proposed degradation pathways of SMX over S-5 sample.
Summary of the physicochemical properties of all the samples.

head.ce:title: Introduction of porous structure via facile carbon-dot modulation: A feasible and promising approach for improving the photocatalytic capability of sulfur doped g-C3N4

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Key Laboratory of Water Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Key Laboratory of Water Pollution Control and Resource Reuse
School of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: g-C3N4 with uniformly porous structure is very attractive owing to its special surface and interface properties in photocatalytic field. Herein, uniformly porous sulfur doped C3N4/carbon dot (S-C3N4/C-dot) composite with narrow pore size distribution was successfully synthesized via a facile one-step calcination of S-C3N4 precursor with the prefabricated C-dot, rather than employing any other template materials. Experiments found, it was the C-dot that served as the pore-forming agent via its own oxidation into gaseous products, and ratio of C-dot in the raw material played a key role in controlling the crystal structure, morphology and photocatalytic property of the final products. With the optimum ratio, the obtained S-C3N4/C-dot composite exhibited a superior activity in antibiotics degradation compared with pure S-C3N4, which was attributed to the unique pore structure, large specific surface area, strong visible light absorption and less recombination of electron-hole pairs. In addition, trapping experiments and intermediate product analyses were also carried out to understand the antibiotics removal mechanism and pathways. This study is very helpful to deeply understand the function of C-dot decoration, and declare the great potential of g-C3N4 in practical water purification.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, photocatalytic technology has been regarded as an effective approach to solve the energy depletion and environmental pollution problems [1]. In order to obtain high efficiency, the critical step is to choose the suitable semiconductor photocatalysts. Compared to the traditional photocatalysts, like TiO2, the promising metal-free photocatalyst, g-C3N4, has received much attention due to its visible light response, low-cost and high stability [2]. However, relatively small surface area, rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole and relatively weak visible light absorption still restrict the practical application of g-C3N4. In order to address the weaknesses, various attempts have been reported, such as metal or non-metal doping [3,4], carbon materials decoration [5,6], coupling with other semiconductor to form heterojunction [7,8] and modulating the morphology of g-C3N4 [9] etc.

Sulfur doping, as a new method to modulate g-C3N4, has showed some potential advantages [10]. Since the similar electronegativities of sulfur and nitrogen, it is feasible and favorable to substitute the edge of N atom in g-C3N4 with S atom, which was in line with the first-principles DFT studies [11]. The research made by Lu et al. had found that sulfur doping distinctly tuned the optical and electronic properties of g-C3N4 and the product showed high efficiency in the reduction of UO2 2+ [10]. In our previous study [8], we synthesized a sulfur doped g-C3N4 through one-step calcination of thiocyanuric acid with melamine, and found that the non-metal sulfur doping could successfully regulate the morphology and alter the electronic structure of g-C3N4. However, the as-prepared S-C3N4 still displayed some drawbacks, such as relatively low specific surface area and inferior separation of photoinduced charge carriers. Therefore, it is necessary to further improve the property of S-C3N4 by some feasible and promising methods, which could not only enhance the mass transfer property of the product, but also further retard the carriers’ recombination of S-C3N4.

Many studies prove that porous structure can increase the specific surface area of g-C3N4, but the reported synthesis usually employed template materials, either hard-template, like silica, or soft-template, such as P123 [12,13]. Templates are powerful, however, removal of template usually is complex [14], and sometimes is very dangerous when hydrofluoric acid is needed; furthermore, structure collapse is normally inevitable along with template removal, leading to inferior photocatalytic performance [15]. A feasible and promising method for introducing porous structure to g-C3N4 should be investigated and established.

Carbon quantum dot (C-dot), a novel type of carbon material with a diameter less than 10 nm, exhibits unique optical and electrical features [16,17]. Many researchers have successfully grafted C-dot onto g-C3N4, and obtained very effective products. Li et al. found that g-C3N4 nanosheets with C-dot deposition exhibited higher photocatalytic activity in hydrogen production since C-dot favored the electron trapping and enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs [18]. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. managed to prepare a C-dot decorated g-C3N4 through an impregnation-thermal method, and the product showed great enhancement in phenol photodegradation because of the rapid electron transfer and the extended visible light absorption [19]. Basically, for the function of C-dot, most of the reports focused on the improved optical and electronic properties, such as the enhanced absorption of visible light and transportation of photogenerated carriers [20].

Generally, besides optical and electronic properties, crystal structure and morphology are also crucial to the performance of the photocatalysts, which have been demonstrated by many studies. Dong et al. obtained a g-C3N4 sample with efficient photocatalytic activity, and found it was attributed to the good crystallinity and large specific surface area of the modified g-C3N4 [21]. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. found that, porous structure elaborated in g-C3N4 can not only increase the specific surface area, but also enhance the migration efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, resulting in good performance of the product in phenol degradation [15]. Nevertheless, in the open literature about C-dot modification of g-C3N4, function of C-dot has not been elucidated comprehensively. Besides optical and electrical investigation, no systematic study about the effects of C-dot modulation on the material morphology (eg. pore structure) and crystallinity has been carried out.

In this study, a C-dot decorated S-C3N4 was successfully prepared using a facile one-step calcination of S-C3N4 precursor with prefabricated C-dot. Interestingly, besides the improved optical and electrochemical properties as reported previously, the obtained S-C3N4/C-dot composite exhibited uniformly porous structure without employing any other template materials. Detailed characterizations indicated that, it was the C-dot, which served as the pore-forming agent, thus increasing the specific surface area of S-C3N4 greatly. Meanwhile, the ratio of C-dot introduction exhibited great influence on modulating the morphology, crystallinity, optical and electrical properties of the final products. Along with the extended visible light response range, fast charge transportation, the obtained S-C3N4/C-dot composite showed remarkable sulfamethoxazole (SMX) removal capability under visible light.

All chemical reagents employed in our work were of analytical grade and used without purification. Glucose, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, melamine (MT) and thiocyanuric acid (TTCA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

S-C3N4 precursor was synthesized by dissolving TTCA (0.8865 g) and MT (0.6306 g) into 100 ml and 50 ml hot water (80–100 °C), respectively. After that, the above two solutions were blended and stirred for 30 min before being transferred into an autoclave (200 ml) under 100 °C for 4 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the mixture was washed by deionized water for several times, and dried at 80 °C to obtain the S-C3N4 precursor.

C-dot was prepared by the alkaline-assisted carbonation of glucose under ultrasonic treatment. Briefly, 9 g glucose and 0.8 g sodium hydroxide were added into 50 ml deionized water and stirred for several minutes. After that, the mixed solution was sonicated for 90 min, and then pH of the solution was adjusted to neutral by hydrochloric acid. Subsequently, the obtained solution was dialyzed for one day before drying to gain the C-dot.

S-C3N4/C-dot composite was prepared by calcining the mixture of S-C3N4 precursor and C-dot. Detailed procedures were as follows: a certain amount of S-C3N4 precursor with different volume of C-dot solution (0/1/3/5/8 ml, 2.1 g/l) was added into 50 ml water followed by stirring for 30 min. The obtained yellow solution was dried at 80 °C first, and then calcined in a furnace at 550 °C for 4 h. The obtained products were labeled as S–X in the text, where X was the volume of C-dot added, and the corresponding ratio of C-dot to S-C3N4 precursor for each sample was 0.15%:1 for S-1, 0.45%:1 for S-3, 0.75%:1 for S-5, and 1.2%:1 for S-8, respectively.
The synthesis procedure of S-C3N4/C-dot composites was schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 as well.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the composites were studied by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) source. The morphology of the as-prepared samples was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 F). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) spectra was obtained by Nicolet Nexus 470 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements was performed using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi equipped with standard monochromatic Al Kα excitation source (hν = 1486.6 eV), and all binding energies were set to C 1 s signal at 284.8 eV for calibration. The specific surface area as well as pore size distribution of the samples were measured by JWGB JW-BK122 W automated pore size and surface area analyzer. The optical property of samples was evaluated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance pattern with a spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The photocurrent and Mott-Schottky plot were measured using Shanghai Chenhua 760E electrochemical workstation, with platinum wire and Ag/AgCl electrode serving as the counter and reference electrode, respectively.

The decomposition of sulfamethoxazole (SMX) was tested to assess the performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts. The light source was a 300 W Xeon lamp with a visible light cutoff filter to make sure λ > 420 nm. The dosage of photocatalysts was 1 g/l and the concentration of SMX was 10 mg/L. Before the light on, the suspension was stirring for 60 min in the dark to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The samples were then withdrawn at given time intervals and filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane filter.

Radical species trapping experiments were also carried out using a parallel procedure as the photocatalytic reaction of SMX degradation, except the addition of 10.0 mM of disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate dihydrate (EDTA-2Na), benzoquinone (BQ), or isopropanol (IPA) to quench holes h+,
O2 − and
OH radicals, respectively.

In this study, SMX concentration was determined by an UltiMate 3000 HPLC analysis (Thermo Scientific, USA Kyoto, Japan) with a 2.1 × 150 mm Atlantis C18 column and the test conditions were as follows: the mobile phase was a mixing solution of water and acetonitrile (60:40 (v/v)) at a constant flow rate of 1 ml/min, and the wavelength was set at 275 nm. HPLC-MS equipped with a LCQ-Fleet mass spectrometer (Thermo Finnigan) was employed to identify the SMX photodegradation intermediates with ESI in negative ion mode over a mass range of 50–400 m/z.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-synthesized samples were exhibited in Fig. 2 . It can be found that there are two peaks at 2θ = 13.4° and 27.5° displayed in all samples. The former peak could be assigned to (100) diffraction planes of g-C3N4, which is the interplanar structural packing; the latter one was attributed to the (002) diffraction plane of g-C3N4, resulted from the stacking of the conjugated aromatic system [22]. Meanwhile, the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1) of all the samples showed some typical peaks at around 807 cm−1, 1200˜1600 cm−1 and 3210 cm−1, respectively. The first peak was attributed to the breathing mode of the tri-s-triazine ring system, and the middle peaks group reflected the characteristic stretching vibration modes of CN and C–N heterocycles [23], while the peak at 3210 cm−1 could be assigned to the terminal NH2 or NH stretching vibration [24]. Both XRD and FTIR results indicated the g-C3N4 was successfully fabricated in the composite [25].

It should be pointed out that, owing to the small amount of sulfur doping and C-dot addition, no obvious sulfur and carbon peaks could be identified in both XRD and FTIR spectra, thus, XPS analysis was performed to further investigate the surface chemical composition of the samples. As shown in Fig. 3 , survey spectrum of S-5 sample exhibited distinct peaks of carbon and nitrogen, with a weak oxygen peak at around 503 eV, which was mainly attributed to the absorbed water. For detail information, the high resolution of C1 s spectra (Fig. 3b) of both S-0 and S-5 displayed three characteristic peaks at 284.8, 288.4 and 293.9 eV, which could be attributed to the sp2 CC bonds, carbon in NCN, and carbon connected to the terminal amino group of g-C3N4 [26,27], respectively. In addition, the peak at 284.8 eV of S-5 is obviously much stronger than that of S-0, which could be attributed to the increasing amount of sp2 CC bonds, indicating the successful coupling of C-dot to g-C3N4 [28]. It should be note that, no peaks corresponding to CS band could be found in C 1 s spectrum, due to the low content of sulfur doping, however, as shown in Fig.3c, in S 2p spectrum of S-5, a peak near 163.9 eV could be identified, which is related to CS bond, and indicated the replacement of sulfur with lattice nitrogen in S-5 [29]. In addition, the N 1 s spectrum of S-5 (Fig. 3d) displayed four deconvoluted peaks at 398.6, 400.1, 401.3 and 404.4 eV, which could be attributed to the sp2-hybridized nitrogen (CNC), tertiary nitrogen N–(C3), nitrogen in the terminal amino group (NH) [30,31] and π-excitations in g-C3N4, respectively. Thus, XPS analysis confirmed the successful formation of S-C3N4/C-dot composite.

Interestingly, with the introduction of C-dot, XRD patterns of all the samples displayed an obviously stronger (002) peak in comparison with S-0 sample. The higher packing density can be ascribed to the electrons localization and stronger layers binding [32], which reflects that the introduction of C-dots could promote the polymerization of g-C3N4 precursor and the crystallization of g-C3N4 nanosheets [33]. It is worthy to note that, with the C-dot addition increasing from 1 ml to 8 ml during the synthesis, (002) peak intensity of the corresponding sample increased firstly, and reached a maximum when C-dot amount was 5 ml (S-5 sample), then decreased with bigger C-dot amount, indicating that an optimum ratio of C-dot to S-C3N4 existed to obtain the superior mature crystal phase. In addition, compared to pure S-C3N4 sample (S-0), characteristic (002) peaks of all the other samples shifts lightly to lower degree, which may be resulted from the introduction of defects induced by C-dot addition [20]. It has been reported that defects are helpful for heterogeneous photocatalytic reactions, since they can serve as active sites for reactant molecules [11]. Thus, S-C3N4/C-dot composites are supposed to good enhanced photocatalytic activity.

For S-0 sample, pores could be scarcely identified in the TEM observation (Fig. 4b), which is consistent with the results of N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, indicating the pore cannot be formed without C-dot addition. Nevertheless, after adding C-dot, porous structures appeared with different microstructure of the pores under various C-dot added ratios. Obviously, compared to S-5, less but bigger irregular pores existed in S-1 and S-3 samples (Fig. 4c & d), and the pores size mainly distributed in a wide range of 3–100 nm. For S-5 sample, both TEM observation (Fig. 4e) and pore size distribution curve indicated that, this sample possessed abundant honeycomb-like pores with size centrally distributed in 2–40 nm, which exactly falls in the mesopore category. Generally, a great number of mesopores would give the sample with large specific surface area [34]. As expected, S-5 possessed the largest specific surface area among all the samples, reaching a value of 98 m2/g, which is three times bigger than that of pure S-0. In addition, it is worthy to note that, for S-8 sample, both N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curve were nearly identical to S-0 sample. Combined with its TEM image (Fig. 4f), it can be easily concluded that few pores existed in S-8, even when its C-dot addition was more than S-5 sample. Thus, all the above analyses indicated that, C-dot introduction along with its addition amount, played vital roles in controlling the microstructures of the samples. No matter too many or too few of C-dot addition, no abundant mesopores would be formed in the final product, while, optimum ratio of C-dot to S-C3N4 precursor in this study was 0.75%:1 (5 ml C-dot addition), which is coincident with XRD analysis.

As mentioned in the experimental part, calcination process was employed during the composite synthesis. We analyzed the mass loss during the calcination for all the samples with results listed in Table 1 , and found that, with the increment of C-dot addition, a parabola going upwards existed with S-5 as the turning point, similar to the XRD, TEM and N2 adsorption-desorption analyses. It is reported that, sulfur could serve as template material to synthesize porous g-C3N4, since sulfur could sublime easily under high temperature and produce pores in the substrate [35]. During the calcination, C-dot could be oxidized into gaseous compounds by the oxygen trapped in the reaction vessel, as evidenced by the obvious mass loss during the calcination. Thus, it is reasonable to suppose that, in this study, it was the release of gaseous compounds produced by C-dot oxidation that form the pores and modulate the microstructure of the final products. Moreover, since porous structure could promote gas exchange, including oxygen entrance and gaseous products release, the produced holes would also accelerate the thermal polymerization of S-C3N4 precursor, and further facilitate the crystallization of g-C3N4 nanosheets, which has been confirmed in the XRD analysis. In addition, in accordance with TEM observation, with the increment of C-dot addition, number of pores increased; but if too many C-dot were added, the limited oxygen trapped in the covered reaction vessel would restrict the oxidation of the C-dot buried in the interior, leading to the failure of pore formation; furthermore, excessive consumption of oxygen would even inhibit the thermal polymerization of S-C3N4 precursor, leading to inferior crystallinity, as for S-8 sample. Thus, it can be concluded that, it is the C-dot that served as the pore-forming agent during the synthesis, and its adding amount modulated the crystal structure and morphology of the final products.

Above discussion demonstrated that introduction of C-dot did impose great impact on morphology and crystal structure of the S-C3N4 substrate. Beyond that, it has also been reported that, C-dot coupling could alter the optical and electrochemical properties of g-C3N4 substrate [19,36], and these were also confirmed in this study with the DRS and transient photocurrent data shown in Figs. 6 and 7 ,.

In Fig. 6, agree well with their dark color, all the modified samples exhibited stronger visible light absorption than S-0, indicating that C-dot decoration did can enhance the light harvesting capability of the products [37]. In addition, it is worthy to note that, after the addition of C-dot, obvious red shifts of light absorption onset could be observed compared with pure S-C3N4, and bandgap of the samples, calculation by employing Kubelka-Munk function, became narrower along with the increment of C-dot addition until 5 ml, as listed in Table 1. It is reported that, bandgap reduction might be resulted from the appearance of additional electronic state above the valence band (VB) of S-C3N4 induced by C-dot introduction [38,39].

To further investigate the electronic structure of S-C3N4/C-dot composite, Mott-Schottky analysis was performed to the typical S-0 and S-5 samples. As shown in Fig. 6c, positive slope of the plots mirrored the n-type semiconductors nature of the samples; therefore, conduction band (CB) position of the samples could be calculated from the intercept of the plots, with values located at -1.60 V and -0.90 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), that is, -1.37 V and -0.67 V (vs. NHE) for S-0 and S-5, respectively. Based on their bandgap values calculated above, the valence band position of S-0 and S-5 were further calculated and located at 1.43 and 2.04 V (vs. NHE), respectively. Thus, introduction of C-dot, like in S-5 sample, did can alter the band structure of S-C3N4, and produce more and stronger oxidizing holes to promote the photocatalytic reaction [40,41].

Retarding the electron-hole recombination is extremely favorable for photocatalytic process, and many carbon materials, such as graphene, carbon nanotube, C-dot, has been proved to be effective in promoting charge transfer, thus further improving the photocatalytic efficiency [42,36]. In this study, the transient photocurrent response of all the samples was recorded to evaluate their charge migration behavior, with results shown in Fig. 7. Consistent with the former section, S-5 remains the most eye-catching sample, with the strongest photocurrent response value even around three times bigger than pure S-C3N4, reflecting its high efficiency in carriers separation and transportation with help of C-dot, since CB of S-5 is more negative than that of C-dot (-0.5 V) [42].

In accordance with the findings in Section 3.1, Fig. 7 also pointed out that, amount of C-dot introduction is critical for the final product, where neither too many nor too few could work well, but may even bring in some adverse effects. For example, S-8 with too many C-dot coupling, behaved even inferior to S-0 in transient photocurrent response, which resulted from many factors, for example, light exposure of S-C3N4 substrate etc., and would be discussed later.

To test the photocatalysis activity of the as-synthesized photocatalysts, the SMX, a commonly used antibiotic, was selected as the pollutant [43]. According to the blank experiment, there was almost no decomposition of SMX without photocatalysts, suggesting the refractory nature of SMX under visible light irradiation. While in Fig. 8 , after adding the as-prepared photocatalysts at dosage of 1 g/L, the degradation rate of SMX was significantly enhanced. As expected, the star product S-5, exhibited superior capability with almost 90% SMX removal achieved within 3 h, followed by S-3 and S-1 samples. However, surprisingly, the S-8 sample, with large amount of C-dot decoration, exhibited inferior activity (<50% removal in 3 h) even to pure S-C3N4 (S-0).

Different performances can be attributed to many factors. Just as discussed before, C-dot coupling did show great influence on morphology, optical and electrochemical properties of the final products; but most importantly, C-dot amount is the determinant factor to direct the ultimate effect, to be positive or negative. Therefore, S-5 sample, with the optimum C-dot ratio in this study, which possessed large specific surface area, uniformly porous structure, mature crystal phase, strong light absorption, efficient charge separation, unsurprisingly, performed best in the photocatalytic reaction; while, S-1 and S-3 samples, neither of which had sufficient C-dot addition, could not compete with S-5 definitely. More profoundly, besides the disadvantage of morphology, too many C-dot coupling in S-8 sample may cover the surface of S-C3N4, and limit its exposure to light [44], thus reducing the amount of photogenerated electron-holes pairs, and further suppressing the SMX degradation. Therefore, different photocatalytic performances reconfirmed that, ratio of C-dot to S-C3N4 was vital for the properties of the final product.

Good stability is another basic requirement for photocatalyst in practical application. To evaluate the stability of S-5 sample, the reusability experiment was carried out. As presented in Fig. 9 , the removal rate of SMX showed slight deactivation after four cycles’ reaction, indicating the S-5 sample possessed good stability, which is favorable for the practical application of the photocatalyst.

In order to identify the active species during the photocatalytic reaction, the scavengers, EDTA-2Na, BQ or IPA was added to the reaction solution to trap the h+,
O2 − and
OH, respectively, with S-5 as the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 10 , the biggest inhibition occurred when BQ was added, which implies that the ·O2 − played a dominant role in SMX degradation. Meanwhile, EDTA-2Na addition also induced a decrease in SMX removal to a certain extent, suggesting that h+ also was the active species. Nevertheless, little decline of SMX removal rate was observed when IPA was added, implying that ·OH was not the main contributor to the SMX photodegradation.

Based on the above results, a rational mechanism of photocatalytic reaction was proposed in Fig. 11 . Once S-C3N4 absorbs the incident visible light, electrons would be excited and jump into the conduction band, and subsequently, transfer to the tightly coupled C-dot rapidly, rather than jump back and recombine with the holes, as evidenced by the transient photocurrent measurement. Then, the electrons would be consumed by the adsorbed O2 and produce
O2 − radicals, which act as the main reactive species to degrade SMX. At the same time, the holes left in the VB of S-C3N4 would directly oxidize SMX as well.

It is worthy to note that, trapping experiments pointed out that ·OH radicals also participated in the photocatalysis. Generally, VB of the common g-C3N4 is not strong enough to oxidize H2O or OH− into
OH [8], nevertheless, as discussed previously, the wonderful S-5 sample possessed very strong VB at 2.04 V, which is strong enough to oxidize OH− into
OH radicals [45], therefore,
OH could be identified in the trapping experiments. But since VB holes could oxidize SMX directly, and concentration of OH− was limited in SMX solution with pH < 7,
OH production got restricted, therefore,
OH could only serve as a minor contributor to the SMX degradation. Meanwhile, consumption of holes by OH− reduced the concentration of holes available, and lowered its contribution to SMX degradation compared to the
O2 − radicals.

MS and HPLC-MS analyses were employed to identify the main intermediates and understand the possible degradation pathway of SMX over S-5 sample, with the observed peaks illustrated in Fig. S2. Nine intermediates could be identified in the MS analysis, which are denoted as P1 (m/z = 282), P2 (m/z = 268), P3 (m/z = 269), P4 (m/z = 284), P5 (m/z = 269), P6 (m/z = 171), P7 (m/z = 286), P8 (m/z = 269) and P9 (m/z = 156) in the text, respectively. Based on the analysis, two possible pathways were proposed and illustrated in Fig. 12 : one involved the oxidation of amine group connected to the benzene ring, and the other started from the hydroxylation of isoxazole ring.

Amine group connected to the benzene ring was vulnerable to be attacked by radical species, thus P1/P2 was supposed to be the oxidation product induced by the
O2 −/
OH attack, respectively. After that, continuous oxidation by
OH radicals happened to P2, with P4 produced when its isoxazole ring being attacked, and P5 appeared when –NH−OH was replaced by −OH. Finally, cleavage of C–N bond occurred, and P6 was generated with lose of isoxazole ring from P5. In the hydroxylation of isoxazole ring pathway, firstly, P3 stemmed from ·OH attacks to the isoxazole ring of SMX; subsequently,
OH attack on the double bond of the isoxazole ring could produce P7 which was a di-hydroxylated derivative of SMX [46]. After that, when P7 was further attacked by radicals, cleavage and lose of the isoxazole ring occurred, leading to the sequential formation of P8 and P9. Finally, all these intermediates would be oxidized and mineralized to CO2, H2O and mineral acids.

It is first reported that, C-dot could serve as pore-forming agent when coupling with S-C3N4 to produce porous S-C3N4/C-dot composite, meanwhile, C-dot amount played a vital role in controlling the morphology, optical and electrochemical properties of the products. With optimum coupling ratio, the obtained S-C3N4/C-dot composite exhibited large specific surface area, uniformly porous structure, mature crystal phase, strong visible light absorption, efficient charge separation and transportation, and performed well in photocatalytic removal of antibiotics. Neither too many nor too few C-dot addition could work well, but even bring in some adverse effects. In addition,
O2 − and valence band holes were the main active species during the SMX degradation, and the degradation pathway was proposed as well. This study may enlighten the future research that, to maximize the potential of C-dot, attention should also be paid to its morphology and microstructure modulation capability, besides optical and electrical properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21607095), the Research Foundation for the Outstanding Young and Middle-aged Scientists of Shandong Province of China (BS2014HZ005), and the Taishan Scholar Program (ts201511003).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Mater. Chem. A Mater. Energy Sustain.
J. Mater. Chem. A Mater. Energy Sustain.
